title-group.article-title: The Impact of COVID‐19 on Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases: A Narrative Review of Long‐Term Consequences and Vaccination Strategies

aff.named-content: Clinical Research Development Unit, Afzalipour Hospital
Thoracic Research Center, Imam Khomeini Hospital Complex
Rasool Akram Hospital Clinical Research Development Center, School of Medicine, Rasool Akram Medical Complex

sec.title: Background and Aims
sec.p: The COVID‐19 pandemic has posed significant challenges for individuals with obstructive pulmonary diseases (OPDs), such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Emerging evidence highlights the complex interplay between SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and OPDs, including increased risks of severe disease, long‐term sequelae, and potential new‐onset respiratory conditions. This narrative review synthesizes current knowledge on the pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical outcomes, and management strategies for COVID‐19 in OPD patients, with a focus on long‐term consequences and vaccination efficacy.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A comprehensive literature search was conducted using PubMed, Embase, and Scopus (January 2020–June 2025). Inclusion criteria encompassed original studies, reviews, and meta‐analyses examining COVID‐19's impact on asthma or COPD, while non‐English publications, animal studies, and case reports were excluded. Data were extracted and thematically synthesized to address acute outcomes, long COVID, vaccination, and therapeutic considerations.

list-item.p: Risk and Severity: COPD patients faced higher risks of severe COVID‐19 (hospitalization, ICU admission, and mortality), while asthma phenotypes influenced outcomes (eosinophilic asthma conferred milder disease).
Long COVID: Persistent respiratory symptoms (dyspnea and cough), fatigue, and cognitive dysfunction were prevalent in OPD patients, with new‐onset asthma reported post‐infection.
Vaccination: mRNA vaccines significantly reduced severe outcomes in OPDs, though biologic therapies in asthma may attenuate immune responses.
Treatment: Antivirals (e.g., nirmatrelvir–ritonavir) and corticosteroids were effective, but drug interactions (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors) required careful management.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: COVID‐19 exacerbates OPD‐related morbidity, underscoring the need for tailored prevention (vaccination and early diagnosis) and multidisciplinary management. Future research should prioritize phenotype‐specific therapeutic strategies and long‐term surveillance to mitigate post‐COVID sequelae in this vulnerable population.

def.p: acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
global initiative for chronic obstructive lung disease

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Obstructive pulmonary diseases (OPDs), primarily chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma, are recognized as major global health problems [1, 2, 3]. These chronic conditions are characterized by airflow limitation that impairs lung function and diminishes quality of life [1, 2, 3].

The COVID‐19 pandemic, driven by the SARS‐CoV‐2 virus, has profoundly affected individuals with OPDs [4, 5, 6]. This virus primarily targets the respiratory system, using the angiotensin‐converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor for cellular entry [7]. Given that ACE2 is highly expressed in lung tissue, the lungs are a primary site for viral replication and inflammation [7]. Patients with OPDs, whose lungs are already characterized by chronic inflammation and compromised function, are therefore more susceptible to the adverse effects of COVID‐19, including severe disease progression and long‐term complications [8].

The emergence of “Long COVID,” a syndrome defined by persistent symptoms like fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and shortness of breath, has amplified these concerns, particularly for vulnerable groups such as those with chronic respiratory diseases [9]. As the understanding of Long COVID evolves, it is crucial to investigate its specific impact on individuals with underlying asthma and COPD. A comprehensive understanding of the complex interplay between OPDs and COVID‐19 is essential for optimizing patient care and developing effective strategies to mitigate the pandemic's impact on this population.

The motivation for this narrative review stems from the need to synthesize the extensive yet fragmented evidence regarding the interaction between COVID‐19 and OPDs, aiming to provide a clear and actionable overview for clinicians and researchers. This narrative review comprehensively assesses the multifaceted impact of COVID‐19 on individuals with OPDs, focusing on the complex interplay of acute and long‐term consequences, and evaluates the critical role of vaccination and therapeutic strategies in mitigation, as visually summarized in Figure 1.

caption.p: The intertwined path of COVID‐19 and obstructive pulmonary diseases (OPDs): challenges and protection. This graphical abstract illustrates the multifaceted impact of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection on individuals with pre‐existing obstructive pulmonary diseases, such as asthma and COPD. The virus exacerbates underlying lung conditions, leading to severe acute disease, persistent Long COVID symptoms (e.g., dyspnea and fatigue), and the potential for new‐onset asthma. Critically, vaccination strategies and tailored treatment approaches serve as essential protective measures to mitigate these adverse outcomes in this vulnerable population.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This narrative review comprehensively evaluated the impact of COVID‐19 on OPD patients, focusing on pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical outcomes, and therapeutic strategies. A systematic literature search was conducted following best practices for narrative reviews.

sec.title: Search Strategy
sec.p:
Electronic databases (PubMed, Embase, and Scopus) were searched from January 2020 to June 2025 using Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) and keywords covering:
OPD terms: COPD, asthma, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, and obstructive lung disease.
COVID‐19 terms: SARS‐CoV‐2, coronavirus, and COVID‐19 infection.
Outcome terms: hospitalization, mortality, ICU admission, long COVID, post‐COVID conditions, and new‐onset asthma.
Intervention terms: COVID‐19 vaccine, vaccination, corticosteroids, antiviral therapy, and pulmonary rehabilitation.

list-item.p: Study designs: Original research, systematic reviews, and meta‐analyses.
Population: Human subjects with pre‐existing asthma or COPD.
Focus: COVID‐19 interactions with OPDs (pathophysiology, outcomes, and management).
Language: English publications.
Non‐human studies.
Articles without extractable OPD‐specific outcome data.
Non‐English publications.
Case reports with fewer than five patients.

sec.title: Data Extraction and Synthesis
sec.p:
Two independent reviewers screened titles/abstracts (inter‐rater reliability κ = 0.82), with disagreements resolved by a third adjudicator. Full texts of eligible articles were reviewed. Data extraction documented:
Study characteristics (design, population, and location).
COVID‐19 outcomes (severity, mortality, and long‐term sequelae).
Therapeutic interventions and responses.
Standardized forms ensured consistent data capture. Narrative synthesis organized findings by thematic domains: pathophysiology, acute outcomes, long COVID, vaccination, and treatment considerations.

sec.title: Obstructive Pulmonary Disease and COVID‐19 Risk
Asthma and SARS‐CoV‐2 Infection
sec.p: The COVID‐19 pandemic has significantly impacted individuals with chronic respiratory conditions, including asthma. While the initial concerns about increased susceptibility to SARS‐CoV‐2 infection in asthmatic patients have largely been alleviated, the infection's impact on asthma outcomes remains a subject of ongoing investigation [10].

sec.title: Pathophysiological Aspects
sec.p: Asthma phenotypes play a pivotal role in determining COVID‐19 severity. Eosinophilic asthma, often managed with biologics and inhaled corticosteroids (ICS), is generally associated with milder disease and better outcomes [5, 11, 12]. This phenotype is characterized by type‐2 inflammatory pathways involving cytokines like IL‐4, IL‐5, and IL‐13. These cytokines downregulate the expression of the ACE2 receptor, a key entry point for SARS‐CoV‐2, potentially conferring some protection against severe COVID‐19 [13, 14, 15].

Conversely, non‐eosinophilic asthma, characterized by neutrophilic inflammation, lacking this protective mechanism and is linked to a higher risk of severe disease and long‐term COVID‐19 symptoms [16, 17]. This phenotype is associated with higher ACE2 receptor expression, increasing susceptibility to adverse COVID‐19 outcomes.
ICS may further mitigate the risk of severe COVID‐19 by reducing ACE2 and transmembrane protease serine 2 (TMPRSS2) expression in the respiratory tract [18, 19].

sec.title: Risk of SARS‐CoV‐2 Infection in Asthmatic Patients
sec.p: Some studies have examined the vulnerability of asthmatic individuals to SARS‐CoV‐2 infection, yielding varying results dependent on the severity of pre‐existing asthma and treatment methods. Research suggests that well‐managed asthma does not significantly elevate the risk of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. However, individuals with severe asthma, especially those on systemic corticosteroids, may have an increased risk due to immunosuppressive effects [15, 20, 21].

sec.title: COVID‐19 Outcomes in Asthmatic Patients
sec.p: The severity of asthma significantly impacts COVID‐19 outcomes. Asthmatic patients on moderate to high doses of ICS may have increased risks of hospitalization and mortality following SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Additionally, patients with asthma exacerbations or oral corticosteroid use exhibit higher hospitalization rates [15, 20, 21]. Nevertheless, well‐controlled asthma patients have mortality rates similar to the non‐asthmatic population [22], emphasizing the importance of asthma control in COVID‐19 outcomes. A substantial proportion of asthmatic individuals infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 experience long COVID symptoms, including respiratory difficulties, increased inhaler use, and impaired asthma control [23, 24]. While some studies suggest an increased risk of new‐onset asthma post‐COVID‐19 infection, particularly among older participants, further confirmation is still needed [15, 23, 24].

sec.title: COPD and SARS‐CoV‐2 Infection
sec.p: The interaction between COPD and COVID‐19 significantly influences the course of the infection and the outcomes. Patients with COPD, due to their basic inflammation in the lungs and disturbed lung function, are thus more prone to severe COVID‐19. This may eventually lead to a more serious course of the disease, increased risks of complications, and generally poor prognosis [25]. Understanding these interrelations is important for developing appropriate preventive and management strategies for such a vulnerable population.

sec.title: Pathophysiological Aspects
sec.p: Patients with COPD are more prone to serious infection with COVID‐19 for a number of reasons. Among the most essential factors are increased ACE2 receptor expressions in the airways of COPD patients, which provide the virus with easy access into the cells [26]. Increased receptor expression, especially in smoker COPD patients [27], significantly influences the higher viral load and greater involvement of respiratory structures [28]. Moreover, the pathophysiology of COPD, with chronic inflammation and obstruction of the airways, can be exacerbated by COVID‐19 [29]. The addition of viral‐induced lung injury to pre‐existing lung damage in these patients can lead to severe respiratory distress that may necessitate admission to the ICU. Furthermore, COPD patients are at increased risk for secondary bacterial infections due to impaired immune function and reduced mucociliary clearance [30]. The triad of COPD, cardiovascular disease (CVD), and COVID‐19 amplifies systemic inflammation through endothelial dysfunction and cytokine storms, while TB coinfection impairs immune responses to SARS‐CoV‐2, accelerating respiratory failure [31, 32, 33].

sec.title: Risk and Impact on Outcome
sec.p: COPD patients are more likely to experience severe COVID‐19, requiring hospitalization, ICU admission, and mechanical ventilation [4, 6, 34, 35]. However, the impact of COPD on mortality from COVID‐19 is less clear. While some studies have shown increased mortality [26, 36], others have not [29, 37]. Factors such as the severity of COPD, smoking history and intensity [38], the presence of comorbidities [6], and access to healthcare may influence these outcomes [39].

The phenotype of COPD also plays a role in COVID‐19 severity. Patients with frequent exacerbations and overlap syndromes, such as asthma‐COPD overlap or COPD‐bronchiectasis, are at higher risk of severe disease [26, 29, 36]. Emphysema‐predominant COPD may be associated with increased risk of ICU admission [36]. Retrospective analyses highlight additional risk factors for severe COVID‐19 in COPD patients, including extensive smoking history (≥ 25 pack‐years), baseline FEV₁ < 50% predicted, and comorbidities like CVD and tuberculosis (TB). COPD‐TB coinfection significantly increases mortality risk (adjusted HR: 1.47; 95% CI: 0.949–2.295), while CVD comorbidity exacerbates poor outcomes in unvaccinated individuals [32, 33].

sec.title: Genetic and Environmental Factors
sec.p: Genetic factors have no significant impact on vulnerability or poor outcomes of COPD patients with COVID‐19 and differences among worldwide populations [40].

sec.title: Therapeutic Considerations
sec.p: Ongoing use of ICS in COPD patients may offer some protection against severe COVID‐19 [41, 42]. Preventive measures, such as vaccination, wearing masks, and practicing good hand hygiene, are crucial for reducing the risk of COVID‐19 infection and severe outcomes in COPD patients [43]. Additionally, timely access to healthcare and remote monitoring through telemedicine can help mitigate the impact of the pandemic on this vulnerable population [39].

sec.title: Post‐COVID‐19 Symptoms in Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases
sec.p: Although acute infections can cause severe respiratory symptoms, the long‐term aftermath of COVID‐19, now widely referred to as “long COVID,” has become a source of great concern for vulnerable populations. Despite growing interest in long COVID, several limitations hinder a definitive understanding of its impact on OPD patients [9]. These include the heterogeneity of study populations, varying definitions of long COVID, and the lack of standardized outcome measures. Studies suggest that individuals with OPDs may experience a similar prevalence of long COVID symptoms compared to the general population. The symptoms post‐COVID‐19 in OPD patients may present as persistent respiratory symptoms, such as shortness of breath, cough, and wheezing, along with systemic symptoms like fatigue, cognitive impairment, and muscle aches [6, 44, 45, 46].

There may be several mechanisms for long‐term COVID‐19 in OPD patients: a direct viral insult to lung tissue leading to residual inflammation and scarring, unresolved inflammation, and dysregulated immune responses could all contribute to chronic respiratory symptoms [47]. Also, psychological factors like anxiety and depression are very likely to increase respiratory symptoms and further deteriorate the health status of a patient [48].

The long‐term consequences of COVID‐19 in OPD patients are not well understood, and further research is required to identify risk factors and develop effective treatment strategies. Large‐scale studies are needed to determine the prevalence, severity, and duration of post‐COVID symptoms in individuals with OPD. Furthermore, studies investigating the underlying mechanisms of long COVID are required, as well as the search for potential biomarkers for early diagnosis and risk stratification [49].

The basis for mitigation of the long‐term impact of COVID‐19 in OPD patients would lie in vaccination programs, mask‐wearing, maintaining social distancing, and proper management of any respiratory ailment. In such conditions, early identification of symptoms in post‐COVID conditions helps avoid further deterioration and increases better patient outcomes [6].

Specific COPD phenotypes further modulate Long COVID risk. Retrospective data from Pakistan identified cough, lower respiratory tract infections (LRTI), and TB history as key predictors of prolonged symptoms (adjusted RR: 1.37 for cough, 0.69 for LRTI; 95% CI: 0.75–1.99), suggesting that baseline exacerbation‐prone COPD predisposes to post‐COVID sequelae [31]. These findings align with the observed 23.4% mortality rate in COPD‐TB coinfected COVID‐19 patients [33], reinforcing the need for targeted monitoring in high‐risk subgroups.

sec.title: Long COVID‌ in Asthmatic Patients
sec.p: Recent studies have highlighted the complex relationship between asthma and long COVID. While some studies suggest an increased risk of severe COVID‐19 in asthmatic patients, particularly those with uncontrolled asthma or comorbidities, others have indicated a potential protective effect of certain asthma phenotypes. For example, patients with T2‐high asthma, defined by high levels of T2 cytokines and eosinophils, might have a reduced risk of severe COVID‐19 and its long‐term consequences. This protective effect may be attributed to the anti‐inflammatory properties of T2 cytokines and the potential antiviral effects of eosinophils [50, 51]. A recent study found that patients with T2‐high asthma had a lower risk of experiencing chest pain after 12 months following COVID‐19 infection [5]. This finding further supports the potential protective role of T2‐high asthma against some of the long‐term sequelae of COVID‐19.

In addition, recent studies are providing new evidence of a link between COVID‐19 infection and the development of new‐onset asthma in adults. One study by Lee et al. demonstrated that the prevalence of new‐onset asthma was higher in patients with a history of COVID‐19 infection [13], which could imply the potential relationship between the virus and asthma development. Supporting this association, a nationwide study by Kim et al., using a large Korean population database, revealed that the risk for new‐onset asthma after COVID‐19 infection was significantly increased [24]. Most interestingly, their study showed that COVID‐19 vaccination seems to decrease the risk of asthma onset and thus might have protective effects on long‐term respiratory complications after COVID‐19 infection.

However, the long‐term consequences of COVID‐19 in asthma patients are poorly understood. Some studies reported persistent respiratory symptoms, including shortness of breath, cough, and wheezing, in individuals with asthma who have recovered from acute COVID‐19 infection [52, 53, 54]. Still, others did not find any significant long‐term impact [15, 55]. The severity of the initial COVID‐19 infection appears to be a crucial factor in determining long‐term outcomes. Patients who experienced severe COVID‐19, including those requiring hospitalization or mechanical ventilation, may be at higher risk of developing long COVID [5]. A recent multicenter cohort study found that having COPD, asthma, and the need for mechanical ventilation during the acute COVID‐19 illness were all significantly associated with persistent dyspnea in patients with long COVID. This highlights the importance of careful monitoring for respiratory issues in asthmatic patients who have recovered from COVID‐19, especially those with severe infections [53].

Besides, the psychological burden of long‐term COVID‐19, including anxiety, fatigue, depression, and cognitive dysfunction, can further exacerbate asthma symptoms and overall quality of life [48]. A recent Japanese study found that asthma was an independent risk factor for persistent fatigue in patients with long COVID‐19. This finding highlights the potential for long COVID‐19 to significantly impact the overall well‐being of asthmatic patients, even beyond the respiratory system [54].

Further studies are needed to understand the effects of COVID‐19 on asthma fully. Large‐scale, well‐designed studies are required to investigate the long‐term impact of COVID‐19 on lung function, quality of life, and asthma control across various asthma phenotypes. Thus, healthcare providers can provide the best possible care for asthma patients' unique challenges in the post‐COVID‐19 era.

The management of long‐term COVID‐19 in asthma could consider pulmonary rehabilitation to improve respiratory function and exercise tolerance; psychological support, considering psychological concerns; pharmacological treatment aimed at the persistence of respiratory symptoms, preventing exacerbations; changing lifestyle toward more physical activities, healthy diet, and stress management [56, 57, 58, 59].

sec.title: Long COVID in COPD ‌Patients
sec.p: Patients with COPD already face chronic inflammation in the lungs and have diminished lung function; thus, they are the most prone to the severe effects of COVID‐19. Long COVID exacerbates their existing respiratory symptoms, which have often been often manifested with frequent exacerbation, reduced lung function, and a tremendous deterioration in the quality of life. These respiratory symptoms usually include shortness of breath, coughing, wheezing, and systemic issues like fatigue, impaired cognition, and body muscle weakness, which highly influence patients' lives [6].

While seasonal coronaviruses are known triggers of acute COPD exacerbations, the classification of COVID‐19 and Long COVID‐19 as exacerbations remains controversial. Based on current definitions, a COPD patient experiencing worsening symptoms like cough and dyspnea requiring additional treatment could be considered to have an exacerbation. However, the underlying pathophysiology of COVID‐19 and Long COVID‐19 differs significantly from typical COPD exacerbations, as evidenced by distinct imaging and post‐mortem findings. This suggests that COVID‐19 and Long COVID‐19 may involve unique disease processes in COPD patients [6, 60].

A holistic approach is necessary to mitigate long‐term COVID‐19 consequences in COPD patients. Emphasis on vaccination and observation of prevention measures, such as wearing masks and social distancing, will help avoid reinfection and decrease the severity of the disease [25]. Early identification and management of Long‐term COVID symptoms are essential in preventing disease progression and improving patient outcomes [61]. Pulmonary rehabilitation can also be very important in restoring respiratory function, exercise tolerance, and quality of life in patients with COPD who now have Long COVID [62]. Along with this, the psychological impact, that is, anxiety and depression, needs to be addressed, also for overall well‐being [48].

With our understanding of Long COVID continuing to evolve, it is here that continuous research is necessary to identify effective strategies for managing this condition among patients with COPD. Investigations on the long‐term impacts of COVID‐19 infection on respiratory function, quality of life, and mortality will require large studies. Addressing these unique challenges faced by patients with COPD infected with Long COVID will surely enhance the patient's life quality and outcomes.

sec.title: COVID‐19 Vaccination in Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases
sec.p: Vaccination remains one of the most important elements of preventive medicine among those suffering from chronic respiratory conditions, including asthma and COPD. Vaccination substantially reduces the risk of infection, hospitalization, and death by preparing the human immune system to recognize and fight against certain pathogens [11, 63]. Notably, vaccination significantly mitigates mortality risk in COPD patients with CVD comorbidities (RR: 0.221; 95% CI: 0.08–0.60), underscoring its protective role in high‐risk subgroups [32].

sec.title: Vaccine Efficacy and Safety in Patients With Asthma
sec.p: Vaccines have demonstrated effectiveness in the asthma population by preventing disease exacerbations and decreasing the frequency and severity of infections, and guidelines generally support their safety [64]. Vaccination remains a critical tool in mitigating the impact of COVID‐19 on asthmatic patients. Both influenza and COVID‐19 vaccines are safe and effective for individuals with asthma [65, 66]. Annual influenza vaccination is strongly recommended, and timely COVID‐19 vaccination, including booster doses, is crucial to protect against severe disease and long‐term complications [66]. COVID‐19 vaccinations have shown considerable efficacy in asthmatic people, with no substantial safety concerns noted.

Data regarding the effectiveness of COVID‐19 vaccines in asthmatic patients receiving biologic agents is conflicting. Some studies suggest a diminished antibody response to SARS‐CoV‐2 mRNA vaccinations in individuals with severe asthma or atopic dermatitis receiving biological treatments [67]. However, other studies have shown no significant impairment of the antibody‐mediated immune response to mRNA vaccines in asthmatic patients on biologics [68]. Healthcare professionals sometimes advise separating vaccinations and biological treatments to mitigate any possible interaction effectively [65, 69]. Further investigations are required to clarify the effect of biological agents on vaccine response in asthmatics. No confirmed reports of disease exacerbations associated with vaccination have been reported among asthmatics. The reassuring safety profile and consistent antibody responses generated in asthma patients, irrespective of biological treatment, increase confidence in administering COVID‐19 vaccines in this population [69]. Ongoing monitoring and compliance with booster vaccine dosages are essential for mitigating exacerbation risk and maintaining enduring immunity [69].

sec.title: COVID‐19 Vaccination in COPD Patients
sec.p: COVID‐19 vaccination is highly recommended for individuals with COPD. mRNA vaccines, such as BNT162b2 and mRNA‐1273, have demonstrated efficacy in reducing the risks of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection, hospitalization, and death in this population [70]. By staying up‐to‐date with vaccinations and following recommended preventive measures, individuals with COPD can significantly reduce their risk of severe COVID‐19 and its associated complications [71]. Although specific studies among COPD patients are limited, the literature on vaccines against COVID‐19 in chronic and immunocompromised conditions has shown the potential to significantly reduce the incidence of symptomatic infection, hospitalization, and death [72]. However, an overall weaker vaccine response is probably expected in COPD patients compared to the general population. Several studies also demonstrated that patients with chronic lung disease often develop lower levels of neutralizing antibodies after vaccination. In this regard, booster vaccinations are highly recommended among people with COPD to lengthen their immunity over some time [71, 72].

sec.title: COVID‐19 Treatment Considerations in Obstructive Pulmonary Diseases
sec.p: Managing COVID‐19 in patients with OPDs is a multidimensional approach. It involves early diagnosis and initiation of appropriate treatment, including timely access to testing and supportive care measures such as oxygen therapy and antiviral medications, if indicated [73]. The patient needs to be kept under observation for the possible development of complications like respiratory failure and secondary bacterial infections [74, 75].

Pharmacological interventions are important in the management of COVID‐19. Available antiviral medications, such as nirmatrelvir–ritonavir (NMV‐r) and molnupiravir (MOL), have shown a decreased risk of hospitalization and severe disease, especially for those at high risk with underlying medical conditions [76]. A recent study from Hong Kong demonstrated NMV‐r may be more effective than molnupiravir in reducing 90‐day mortality and shortening hospital stays in unvaccinated adult patients with chronic respiratory diseases hospitalized for moderate COVID‐19 without hypoxemia on admission [77]. Remdesivir is particularly beneficial, especially when patients have risk factors predisposing them to severe disease [76].

Corticosteroids, particularly for patients requiring hospitalization due to severe COVID‐19, may help reduce inflammation and improve lung function. Systemic glucocorticoids should be started without delay in the case of a COPD exacerbation, as delayed treatment might lead to a serious exacerbation [71]. ICS forms the cornerstone of asthma management and should generally be continued during the COVID‐19 pandemic, with a better balancing of the benefits of ICS in asthma management against the potential risks from the therapy [78]. Interestingly, some studies have suggested a potential protective effect of long‐term corticosteroid use in patients with asthma and COPD against severe COVID‐19. This may be related to the anti‐inflammatory effects of corticosteroids, which could help modulate the hyperinflammatory response associated with severe COVID‐19 [79].

sec.title: Medication Considerations
sec.p: Nebulizers: Early concerns about SARS‐CoV‐2 transmission via nebulization emerged from studies demonstrating the virus's aerosol stability [80]. Theoretical risks suggested nebulizers could aerosolize contaminated particles, potentially exposing healthcare workers and surfaces to infectious droplets [81]. Consequently, stringent safety protocols were widely adopted during the pandemic, including patient masking, expiratory filters, enhanced ventilation, and comprehensive PPE [81].

However, recent clinical evidence challenges these concerns. A controlled study analyzing nebulizers used by hospitalized COVID‐19 patients found no detectable SARS‐CoV‐2 contamination in residual solutions or emitted aerosols, indicating minimal aerosolization risk from the devices themselves [82]. Despite these findings, precautions remain warranted due to independent risks from patient exhalation. When feasible, pressurized metered‐dose inhalers (pMDIs) with spacers or dry powder inhalers (DPIs) are still preferred alternatives to mitigate theoretical transmission risks [81].

Inhaled Corticosteroids: While ICS use may protect against severe COVID‐19, COPD patients on ICS with ≥ 25 pack‐year smoking history face elevated risks of community‐acquired pneumonia (OR: 22.79; 95% CI: 20.41–31.52) and mortality. Careful risk‐benefit assessment is warranted [83].

Biologics: Biologic therapies have revolutionized the treatment of severe asthma in recent years, offering significant benefits for patients with uncontrolled disease. These medications, which target specific components of the immune system, such as interleukin‐5 (IL‐5), interleukin‐4 (IL‐4), interleukin‐13 (IL‐13), immunoglobulin E (IgE), and thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), can effectively reduce airway inflammation, improve lung function, and reduce the frequency and severity of asthma attacks [84]. Regarding COVID‐19, data generated to date generally support the relative safety of these therapies regarding the risk of contracting COVID‐19 infection and the risk of subsequent morbidity/mortality. However, a recent study suggests that patients taking benralizumab, dupilumab, or mepolizumab may have lower antibody and cellular immune responses after receiving a two‐dose mRNA COVID‐19 vaccine than those not. This finding highlights the importance of ongoing research to fully understand the interplay between biological therapies, vaccination, and COVID‐19 [85].

Some intriguing evidence suggests that certain biologics, particularly those targeting IL‐13 (such as dupilumab), may even offer some protection against severe COVID‐19. This may be due to the role of IL‐13 in modulating the inflammatory response to SARS‐CoV‐2 infection [86]. In hospitalized patients with COVID‐19, the anti‐IgE therapy omalizumab was associated with a significant reduction in the risk of progression to mechanical ventilation or death [87]. Although promising, these findings require further investigation in larger trials.

Allergen Immunotherapy: Allergen immunotherapy may exert some immunomodulatory effects that could beneficially influence the course of COVID‐19 [88]. However, more research is needed to define its role in COVID‐19 management and prevention.

sec.title: Drug–Drug Interactions
sec.p: Drug–drug interactions should be considered with OPD drugs in correlation with COVID‐19 medication, particularly with nirmatrelvir–ritonavir, as ritonavir is a powerful inhibitor of the enzyme CYP3A4 [89]. We checked for potential drug interactions between common OPD medications and medications used to treat COVID‐19, using trusted resources, including Micromedex, Lexicomp, and the Liverpool COVID‐19 Drug Interactions Checker (https://www.covid19-druginteractions.org/checker). The findings are summarized in Table 1.

caption.p: Potential drug interactions during COVID‐19 treatment in patients with OPDs.

fn.p: Abbreviations: ICS, inhaled corticosteroids; LABAs, long‐acting beta‐agonists; SABA/LABA, short‐acting beta‐agonist/long‐acting beta‐agonist; SAMA/LAMA: short‐acting muscarinic antagonist/long‐acting muscarinic antagonist.
Green: Likely safe, minimal or no clinically significant interaction expected.
Yellow: Caution advised, potential for a mild interaction. Monitoring may be necessary.
Orange: Moderate interaction risk. Close monitoring and potential dose adjustments may be required.
Red: Significant interaction risk. Avoid concomitant use whenever possible.
Major interactions exist with salmeterol only; no significant interactions were observed with other beta‐agonists.
Potential interaction exists between umeclidinium bromide and imatinib. Imatinib is a weak inhibitor of CYP2D6 and may increase umeclidinium bromide exposure.

sec.title: CYP3A4 Inhibition and Potential Risks
sec.p: Long‐Acting Beta‐Agonists (LABAs): Ritonavir significantly increases blood levels of LABAs such as salmeterol and vilanterol, which are metabolized by CYP3A4. This may increase the risk for cardiac adverse effects, including abnormal heart rhythms, palpitations, and rapid heart rate. Therefore, using nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is not indicated with salmeterol or vilanterol‐containing inhalers. Other LABAs, such as formoterol or olodaterol that are not significantly metabolized by CYP3A4 may be considered [89].

Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS): Many ICS drugs are also metabolized by CYP3A4. Administration of ritonavir with ICS may result in increased systemic steroid exposure, which could lead to adrenal suppression with symptoms such as thinning skin, cataracts, and weight gain. ICS should be used with caution during ritonavir treatment. Systemic steroid side effects were observed in clinical trials; an increased risk was noted with fluticasone‐containing products. Alternative ICS with lesser CYP3A4 metabolism, such as budesonide or mometasone, are preferred [89].

list-item.p: Montelukast: Although primarily metabolized by CYP2C8, montelukast is potentially affected by ritonavir's effect on CYP3A4 [89].
Azithromycin: Occasionally used as an adjunct therapy for asthma, azithromycin also inhibits CYP3A4 [90].
Theophylline: Ritonavir can increase theophylline levels, potentially leading to toxicity. Close monitoring of theophylline levels and dose adjustment may be necessary.

sec.title: Additional Considerations

list-item.p: Potential Weak Interactions: Table 1 also includes potential weak interactions between medications. These interactions are unlikely to cause significant problems but warrant some caution, particularly for patients with underlying health conditions or those taking multiple medications. Examples include combinations like remdesivir with formoterol or extended administration of nirmatrelvir/ritonavir with fluticasone.

Potential Interaction: Fluvoxamine, a medication sometimes used for COVID‐19, may have a potential interaction with formoterol, an LABA. More data is needed to confirm the clinical significance of this interaction.

sec.p: Healthcare providers should meticulously review the medications taken by patients with OPDs and assess for potential drug interactions with COVID‐19 treatments or consult with a clinical pharmacist, particularly those involving CYP3A4 inhibition—Individualize treatment plans based on patient‐specific factors, including asthma severity, medication history, and other comorbidities [91, 92]. Closely monitor patients for any adverse effects during and after treatment with COVID‐19 medications.

sec.title: Obstructive Pulmonary Function Findings in COVID‐19 Survivors
sec.p: While COVID‐19 primarily causes restrictive lung impairments [93, 94, 95, 96, 97], obstructive abnormalities can also occur [96, 97, 98], particularly in those with persistent respiratory symptoms [99, 100]. Studies show that obstructive patterns are observed in a small percentage (5%–9.2%) of recovered COVID‐19 patients, often those who experienced severe pneumonia [97, 98, 101]. Tests sensitive to small airway dysfunction, such as FEF25‐75%, have shown abnormalities in up to 31% of patients with persistent dyspnea, suggesting small airways may contribute significantly to ongoing symptoms [100, 102].

Pulmonary function typically improves over time, with significant recovery observed within 1–3 years [103, 104, 105], although some individuals may experience persistent impairments [106]. COVID‐19 can also increase the risk of new‐onset obstructive airway diseases [107]. Recent evidence points to a significant association between COVID‐19 infection and the development of new‐onset asthma in adults. For instance, a study by Lee et al. demonstrated a higher prevalence of new‐onset asthma in individuals with a prior history of COVID‐19 [13]. This finding is corroborated by a large, nationwide Korean study by Kim et al., which revealed a significantly increased risk for new‐onset asthma following COVID‐19 infection. The study by Kim et al. also found that COVID‐19 vaccination was associated with a decreased risk of developing asthma, suggesting a potential protective role against this specific long‐term respiratory complication [24]. Obstructive impairments appear less prevalent among pediatric and adolescent populations [108]. Follow‐up studies in children and young adults have demonstrated minimal evidence of obstructive patterns, except in severe cases [108]. Similarly, among adults with mild COVID‐19, longitudinal data show negligible changes in obstructive parameters such as FEV1/FVC following recovery [109].

sec.title: Future Directions
sec.p: The proposed roadmap (Table 2) prioritizes six actionable domains to address knowledge gaps in COVID‐19 and OPDs. Longitudinal studies must delineate phenotype‐specific trajectories—particularly the role of eosinophilic inflammation in mitigating Long COVID, as suggested by emerging data [5, 50, 51]. Concurrently, biomarker discovery should leverage advanced omics to identify high‐risk patients, while mechanistic studies must dissect viral persistence in remodeled airways, a hallmark of COPD [28, 47]. Crucially, variant surveillance and therapeutic trials require inclusive designs that account for comorbidities (e.g., CVD [33] and TB [32]), ensuring equitable advances in care. Finally, personalized models integrating digital health tools could revolutionize post‐COVID management for OPD patients globally.

caption.p: A proposed roadmap for future research into the intersection of COVID‐19 and obstructive pulmonary diseases.

list-item.p: How do pre‐existing OPDs (asthma/COPD) alter the 5‐year progression of Long COVID?
Do eosinophilic vs. neutrophilic asthma phenotypes show divergent outcomes?
Guides personalized rehabilitation programs.
Identifies high‐risk subgroups for early intervention.
Can multi‐omics (proteomics, single‐cell RNA‐seq) pinpoint predictive biomarkers for severe COVID‐19/Long COVID in OPDs?
Do ACE2/TMPRSS2 expression patterns correlate with outcomes?
Enables precision diagnostics (e.g., blood tests for risk stratification).
Reveals novel drug targets (e.g., IL‐13 blockade in T2‐high asthma).
Does SARS‐CoV‐2 trigger persistent airway remodeling in COPD via EMT pathways?
Is viral reservoir establishment in OPDs linked to Long COVID?
Develops targeted therapies (e.g., antifibrotics for post‐COVID airway fibrosis).
Clarifies the role of biologics in modulating Long COVID.
How do Omicron subvariants affect exacerbation rates in vaccinated vs. unvaccinated OPD patients?
Does COPD severity (GOLD stage) alter cross‐variant immunity?
Updates vaccination boosters for OPD subgroups.
Tailors inpatient protocols for emerging variants.
Can inhaled IFN‐λ or NLRP3 inhibitors reduce hospitalization in OPDs with acute COVID‐19?
Do pulmonary rehabilitation + IL‐6 antagonists improve Long COVID fatigue in COPD?
Accelerates FDA/EMA approvals for OPD‐specific COVID‐19 therapies.
Reduces systemic steroid dependence.
Can AI‐driven models (e.g., FEV1 + cytokine profiles) predict optimal biologic therapy for post‐COVID asthma?
Do telehealth‐based interventions improve adherence in rural OPD populations?
Lowers healthcare disparities through scalable remote monitoring.
Integrates comorbidities (CVD/TB) into treatment algorithms.

fn.p: Abbreviations: ACE2, angiotensin‐converting enzyme 2; AI, artificial intelligence; COPD, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; CVD, cardiovascular disease; EMA, European Medicines Agency; EMT, epithelial–mesenchymal transition; FDA, Food and Drug Administration; FEV1, forced expiratory volume in 1 s; GOLD, global initiative for chronic obstructive lung disease; IFN‐λ, interferon‐lambda; IL‐6, interleukin‐6; IL‐13, interleukin‐13; Long COVID, post‐acute sequelae of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection; NLRP3, NLR family pyrin domain‐containing 3; OPD, obstructive pulmonary disease; scRNA‐seq, single‐cell RNA sequencing; TB, tuberculosis; T2‐high/T2‐low, type 2‐high/type 2‐low asthma phenotypes; TMPRSS2, transmembrane protease, serine 2.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In conclusion, COVID‐19 has had a significant and multifaceted impact on individuals with OPDs, including asthma and COPD. The virus has exacerbated pre‐existing respiratory conditions, increased the risk of severe illness and long‐term complications, and even triggered the onset of new respiratory issues in some cases. Although vaccination remains one of the most important tools to mitigate COVID‐19, much research activity is still necessary to explore the long‐term effects caused by the virus in individuals suffering from OPDs and thus develop effective ways of their management. Continued prevention, early diagnosis, and appropriate management are critical for improving patients' outcomes and enhancing life quality in people living with OPDs in the post‐COVID‐19 era.
